[[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Tambasasayama, Hyogo)]][[ja:春日神社 (丹波篠山市)]][[ko:가스가 신사 (단바사사야마시)]] [[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Tambasasayama, Hyogo)]][[ja:春日神社 (丹波篠山市)]][[ko:가스가 신사 (단바사사야마시)]] [[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Tambasasayama, Hyogo)]][[ja:春日神社 (丹波篠山市)]][[ko:가스가 신사 (단바사사야마시)]] [[Category:sn_crud commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Tambasasayama, Hyogo)]][[ja:春日神社 (丹波篠山市)]][[ko:가스가 신사 (단바사사야마시)]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Kasuga Jinja (春日神社) | location = {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|Tamba-Sasayama|en|Tamba-Sasayama|WD=Q877169|lt=Tamba-Sasayama|1=Tamba-Sasayama}}黒岡75 | coordinates = {{Coord|35.07825|N|135.21883333333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | enshrined_deities = {{ill|Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|武甕槌命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to タケミカヅチ|1=Takemikazuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1502690}}
{{ill|Futsunushi-no-kami|de|Futsunushi-no-kami|ja|経津主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi-no-kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 経津主神|1=Futsunushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107286}}
{{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|en|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}
{{ill|Hime-Ōkami|de|Hime-Ōkami|ja|姫大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime-Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 比売神|1=Himegami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22070227}} | shrine_Rank = {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|de|Präfekturschrein|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | main_hall = | main_festival = 3rd Saturday and Sunday of {{ill|October|en|October|WD=Q124|lt=October|1=October}} | image = File:篠山春日神社本殿sasayama-kasugajinjaPA156109.jpg | map = {{Location map | Japan Hyōgo | lat_deg = 35 | lat_min = 04 | lat_sec = 41.7 | lon_deg = 135 | lon_min = 13 | lon_sec = 07.8 | label = Kasuga Jinja | position = center | width = 200 | float = center }} | prefecture= {{ill|Tamba-Sasayama|WD=Q877169|1=Tamba-Sasayama}} | founded_by= {{ill|Fujiwara no Mototsune|WD=Q1397196|1=Fujiwara no Mototsune}}; {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|WD=Q2994735|1=Fujiwara no Tokihira}} | deity= {{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}; {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}; {{ill|Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}}; {{ill|Takemikazuchi|WD=Q1502690|1=Takemikazuchi}} }} '''Kasuga Jinja''' (春日神社) is a Shinto shrine located at {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|Tamba-Sasayama|en|Tamba-Sasayama|WD=Q877169|lt=Tamba-Sasayama|1=Tamba-Sasayama}}黒岡75. It was {{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|WD=Q195793|lt=Branch shrine|1=Bunrei}} of the {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga Grand Shrine|1=Kasuga-taisha}} in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}. Under the {{ill|Former shrine Ranking system|de|Ehemaliges Shintō-Rangsystem|ja|旧社格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Former shrine Ranking system|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}, it held the status of a {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|de|Präfekturschrein|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. ==Overview== It enshrines a branch of the {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga Grand Shrine|1=Kasuga-taisha}} of Nara, and locally the deity is known as "Okasuga-san" – an {{ill|Ujigami|en|Ujigami|WD=Q767655|lt=Ujigami|1=Ujigami}}. The spring {{ill|Kasuga Noh|de|Kasuga Nō|ja|春日能|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Noh|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} performed on the {{ill|Noh stage|de|Nō-Bühne|ja|能楽殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Noh stage|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the autumn {{ill|Party|en|Party|WD=Q200538|lt=Festival|1=Party}} held on the third Saturday and Sunday of {{ill|October|en|October|WD=Q124|lt=October|1=October}} are said to be modeled after the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|en|Gion Matsuri|WD=Q979873|lt=Gion Festival|1=Gion Matsuri}} of {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|WD=Q692714|lt=Yasaka|1=Yasaka Shrine}} in Kyoto. In this celebration, four mikoshi (portable shrines) and nine elaborately decorated yamahoko floats parade through an old town that retains the atmosphere of "Little Kyoto" with the resonant sounds of flutes ({{ill|Flute|en|Flute|WD=Q11405|lt=Fue|1=Flute}}) and bells ({{ill|Bell|en|Bell|WD=Q101401|lt=Kane (instrument)|1=Bell}}), evoking a charm regarded as more atmospheric than the modern rendition of Kyoto’s Gion Festival. ==History== In the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, in {{ill|876|en|876|WD=Q23815|lt=876|1=876}} ({{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|WD=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan|1=Zhenguan}} 18), Fujiwara no Mototsune and his son Fujiwara no Tokihira, then lords of Hioki Manor, enshrined a branch of the {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga Grand Shrine|1=Kasuga-taisha}} (located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}) – the guardian deity of the Fujiwara clan – in a small hill in the southern part of Kurooka Village (then known as Sasayama, where {{ill|Sasayama Castle|en|Sasayama Castle|WD=Q2760134|lt=Sasayama Castle|1=Sasayama Castle}} now stands). This is considered the origin of the shrine. Later, in {{ill|1609|en|1609|WD=Q6762|lt=1609|1=1609}} ({{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō|1=Keichō}} 14), during the construction of Sasayama Castle, the shrine was relocated to its present site. The Kanjin-kō, which involved relocating the four {{ill|Shinrei|de|Shinrei|ja|神霊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinrei|ja_comment=jawiki s to 霊魂|1=Soul|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9165}} used for the autumn festival, saw two of these donated from Kurooka Village in {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku|1=Genroku}} 7 during {{ill|June|en|June|WD=Q120|lt=June|1=June}}, while the other two were contributed from Sasayama Town in {{ill|An'ei|en|An'ei|WD=Q1193910|lt=An'ei|1=An'ei}} 8 during {{ill|September|en|September|WD=Q123|lt=September|1=September}} (although there is also a theory placing it in Genroku 13). The nine floats, known as the “Chinchiki-yama,” were donated by various local communities from {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun|1=Kanbun (era)}} 3 onward. They were modeled after the floats of Kyoto’s Gion Festival and are noted for their splendid, luxurious appearance. Until the late {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, floats adorned with motifs such as phoenixes, peacocks, cycads, and shōjō were paraded; however, the advent of {{ill|Electrical cable|en|Electrical cable|WD=Q188447|lt=Electric wires|1=Electrical cable}} eventually necessitated floats without such adornments. In addition, the {{ill|Taiko drum float|de|Taiko-Festwagen|ja|太鼓御興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiko drum float|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is said to have originated during the {{ill|Culture and Bunsei eras|de|Bunka/Bunsei|ja|文化・文政|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Culture and Bunsei eras|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, when potters of {{ill|Ōjiyama ware|de|Ōjiyama-Keramik|ja|王地山焼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōjiyama ware|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} first began carrying it. ;Hoko Mountain [[File:春日神社山鉾巡行PA160707.jpg|thumbnail|180px|Yamahoko Procession (Isakotsu Mountain). On Aoyama Street.]] :It measures 6 meters in length and is approximately 5 meters high; the height up to the {{ill|Roof|en|Roof|WD=Q83180|lt=Roof|1=Roof}} is about two-thirds that of the floats used in Kyoto’s Gion Festival, clearly reflecting Kyoto’s influence. The original Hoko Mountain was smaller and simpler than the current one; however, around the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, it evolved to its present scale and gained in splendor. It is said that the first Hoko Mountain was constructed in {{ill|1663|en|1663|WD=Q7577|lt=1663|1=1663}} ({{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun|1=Kanbun (era)}} 3) on Mikasa Mountain in Kamigawara Town. ({{ill|Tamba-Sasayama municipal designation|de|Städtisches Kulturgut Tamba-Sasayama|ja|篠山市指定文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamba-Sasayama municipal designation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) *Upper Town – 4 units : Mikasa Mountain (Kamigawara Town), Phoenix Mountain (Shimogawara Town), Peacock Mountain (Kamitachi Town), Takasago Mountain (Shimotachi Town) *Lower Town – 5 units : Kenhoko Mountain (Gofukuchō), Shōshō Mountain (Kaminikaimachō), Isakotsu Mountain (Shimonikaimachō), Sotetsu Mountain (Uoyachō), Kagami Mountain (Nishimachō) ;Mikoshi :The mikoshi were donated and enshrined in {{ill|1694|en|1694|WD=Q7694|lt=1694|1=1694}} ({{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku|1=Genroku}} 7) and {{ill|1779|en|1779|WD=Q7714|lt=1779|1=1779}} ({{ill|An'ei|en|An'ei|WD=Q1193910|lt=An'ei|1=An'ei}} 8), respectively. The presence of four mikoshi corresponds to the four festival deities of Kasuga Jinja – namely, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|en|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}, {{ill|Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|武甕槌命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to タケミカヅチ|1=Takemikazuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1502690}}, {{ill|Futsunushi-no-kami|de|Futsunushi-no-kami|ja|経津主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi-no-kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 経津主神|1=Futsunushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107286}}, and {{ill|Himegami|en|Himegami|WD=Q22070227|lt=Hime-Ōkami|1=Himegami}}. In front of Kasuga Jinja. ==Shrine Buildings== * {{ill|Honden|en|Honden|WD=Q2044994|lt=Main Hall|1=Honden}} * {{ill|Noh stage|de|Nō-Bühne|ja|能楽殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Noh stage|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} [[File:春日神社ー篠山ー翁奉納P1011774.jpg|thumbnail|180px|Okina Dedication (2006.1.1)]] :The Noh stage was built in {{ill|1861|en|1861|WD=Q7693|lt=1861|1=1861}} (the first year of {{ill|Bunkyū|en|Bunkyū|WD=Q730266|lt=Bunkyū|1=Bunkyū}}) during the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|WD=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu|1=Bakumatsu}} by Aoyama Tadayoshi, the 13th lord of the Sasayama domain. Known as a Noh enthusiast, he dedicated this stage as an offering. The architectural work was executed by Inayama Kasha and Nagai Rihei, while the pine paintings in the stage background were created by Matsuoka Sōemon; at the time, it was acclaimed as the finest Noh stage west of {{ill|Hakone|de|Hakone|ja|箱根|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakone|1=Hakone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603342}}. To enhance the resonance of footsteps on the floorboards, a large jar was installed underneath—and its perfection is considered among the best in the nation. On {{ill|January 1|en|January 1|WD=Q2150|lt=January 1|1=January 1}} during New Year in Japan, the Okina dedication is held, and every year in early {{ill|April|en|April|WD=Q118|lt=April|1=April}}, when the {{ill|Sakura (disambiguation)|en|Sakura (disambiguation)|WD=Q225112|lt=Sakura|1=Sakura (disambiguation)}} bloom, the "Sasayama Kasuga Noh" is staged. In {{ill|2003|en|2003|WD=Q1986|lt=2003|1=2003}} (Heisei 15), it was designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.篠山市教育委員会 現地説明看板 * {{ill|Ema Hall|de|Ema-Halle|ja|絵馬堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema Hall|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} :Constructed in {{ill|1952|en|1952|WD=Q5272|lt=1952|1=1952}} ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 27) August as a memorial to peace, the Ema Hall houses a horse painting that originally hung in the old prayer hall. In {{ill|Keian|en|Keian|WD=Q1146135|lt=Keian|1=Keian}} 2, Matsudaira Tadakuni (the lord of the Akashi Domain in Harima Province) dedicated a finely detailed Black Divine Horse by Kano Naonobu. Owing to a local legend that at night the horse would escape the painting to devour beans in the Kurooka fields, a metal net was installed exclusively on this ema. This Black Divine Horse has also been designated as a treasured cultural property. ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Property (National)=== * Noh stage ===Tamba-Sasayama Municipal Cultural [[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Tambasasayama, Hyogo)]][[ja:春日神社 (丹波篠山市)]][[ko:가스가 신사 (단바사사야마시)]] [[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Tambasasayama, Hyogo)]][[ja:春日神社 (丹波篠山市)]][[ko:가스가 신사 (단바사사야마시)]] [[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Tambasasayama, Hyogo)]][[ja:春日神社 (丹波篠山市)]][[ko:가스가 신사 (단바사사야마시)]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Tambasasayama, Hyogo)]][[ja:春日神社 (丹波篠山市)]][[ko:가스가 신사 (단바사사야마시)]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Kasuga Jinja (春日神社) | location = {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|Tamba-Sasayama|en|Tamba-Sasayama|WD=Q877169|lt=Tamba-Sasayama|1=Tamba-Sasayama}}黒岡75 | coordinates = {{Coord|35.07825|N|135.21883333333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | enshrined_deities = {{ill|Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|武甕槌命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to タケミカヅチ|1=Takemikazuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1502690}}
{{ill|Futsunushi-no-kami|de|Futsunushi-no-kami|ja|経津主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi-no-kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 経津主神|1=Futsunushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107286}}
{{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|en|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}
{{ill|Hime-Ōkami|de|Hime-Ōkami|ja|姫大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime-Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 比売神|1=Himegami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22070227}} | shrine_Rank = {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|de|Präfekturschrein|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | main_hall = | main_festival = 3rd Saturday and Sunday of {{ill|October|en|October|WD=Q124|lt=October|1=October}} | image = File:篠山春日神社本殿sasayama-kasugajinjaPA156109.jpg | map = {{Location map | Japan Hyōgo | lat_deg = 35 | lat_min = 04 | lat_sec = 41.7 | lon_deg = 135 | lon_min = 13 | lon_sec = 07.8 | label = Kasuga Jinja | position = center | width = 200 | float = center }} | prefecture= {{ill|Tamba-Sasayama|WD=Q877169|1=Tamba-Sasayama}} | founded_by= {{ill|Fujiwara no Mototsune|WD=Q1397196|1=Fujiwara no Mototsune}}; {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|WD=Q2994735|1=Fujiwara no Tokihira}} | deity= {{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}; {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}; {{ill|Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}}; {{ill|Takemikazuchi|WD=Q1502690|1=Takemikazuchi}} }} '''Kasuga Jinja''' (春日神社) is a Shinto shrine located at {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|Tamba-Sasayama|en|Tamba-Sasayama|WD=Q877169|lt=Tamba-Sasayama|1=Tamba-Sasayama}}黒岡75. It was {{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|WD=Q195793|lt=Branch shrine|1=Bunrei}} of the {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga Grand Shrine|1=Kasuga-taisha}} in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}. Under the {{ill|Former shrine Ranking system|de|Ehemaliges Shintō-Rangsystem|ja|旧社格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Former shrine Ranking system|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}, it held the status of a {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|de|Präfekturschrein|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. ==Overview== It enshrines a branch of the {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga Grand Shrine|1=Kasuga-taisha}} of Nara, and locally the deity is known as "Okasuga-san" – an {{ill|Ujigami|en|Ujigami|WD=Q767655|lt=Ujigami|1=Ujigami}}. The spring {{ill|Kasuga Noh|de|Kasuga Nō|ja|春日能|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Noh|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} performed on the {{ill|Noh stage|de|Nō-Bühne|ja|能楽殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Noh stage|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the autumn {{ill|Party|en|Party|WD=Q200538|lt=Festival|1=Party}} held on the third Saturday and Sunday of {{ill|October|en|October|WD=Q124|lt=October|1=October}} are said to be modeled after the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|en|Gion Matsuri|WD=Q979873|lt=Gion Festival|1=Gion Matsuri}} of {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|WD=Q692714|lt=Yasaka|1=Yasaka Shrine}} in Kyoto. In this celebration, four mikoshi (portable shrines) and nine elaborately decorated yamahoko floats parade through an old town that retains the atmosphere of "Little Kyoto" with the resonant sounds of flutes ({{ill|Flute|en|Flute|WD=Q11405|lt=Fue|1=Flute}}) and bells ({{ill|Bell|en|Bell|WD=Q101401|lt=Kane (instrument)|1=Bell}}), evoking a charm regarded as more atmospheric than the modern rendition of Kyoto’s Gion Festival. ==History== In the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, in {{ill|876|en|876|WD=Q23815|lt=876|1=876}} ({{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|WD=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan|1=Zhenguan}} 18), Fujiwara no Mototsune and his son Fujiwara no Tokihira, then lords of Hioki Manor, enshrined a branch of the {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga Grand Shrine|1=Kasuga-taisha}} (located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}) – the guardian deity of the Fujiwara clan – in a small hill in the southern part of Kurooka Village (then known as Sasayama, where {{ill|Sasayama Castle|en|Sasayama Castle|WD=Q2760134|lt=Sasayama Castle|1=Sasayama Castle}} now stands). This is considered the origin of the shrine. Later, in {{ill|1609|en|1609|WD=Q6762|lt=1609|1=1609}} ({{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō|1=Keichō}} 14), during the construction of Sasayama Castle, the shrine was relocated to its present site. The Kanjin-kō, which involved relocating the four {{ill|Shinrei|de|Shinrei|ja|神霊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinrei|ja_comment=jawiki s to 霊魂|1=Soul|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9165}} used for the autumn festival, saw two of these donated from Kurooka Village in {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku|1=Genroku}} 7 during {{ill|June|en|June|WD=Q120|lt=June|1=June}}, while the other two were contributed from Sasayama Town in {{ill|An'ei|en|An'ei|WD=Q1193910|lt=An'ei|1=An'ei}} 8 during {{ill|September|en|September|WD=Q123|lt=September|1=September}} (although there is also a theory placing it in Genroku 13). The nine floats, known as the “Chinchiki-yama,” were donated by various local communities from {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun|1=Kanbun (era)}} 3 onward. They were modeled after the floats of Kyoto’s Gion Festival and are noted for their splendid, luxurious appearance. Until the late {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, floats adorned with motifs such as phoenixes, peacocks, cycads, and shōjō were paraded; however, the advent of {{ill|Electrical cable|en|Electrical cable|WD=Q188447|lt=Electric wires|1=Electrical cable}} eventually necessitated floats without such adornments. In addition, the {{ill|Taiko drum float|de|Taiko-Festwagen|ja|太鼓御興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiko drum float|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is said to have originated during the {{ill|Culture and Bunsei eras|de|Bunka/Bunsei|ja|文化・文政|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Culture and Bunsei eras|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, when potters of {{ill|Ōjiyama ware|de|Ōjiyama-Keramik|ja|王地山焼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōjiyama ware|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} first began carrying it. ;Hoko Mountain [[File:春日神社山鉾巡行PA160707.jpg|thumbnail|180px|Yamahoko Procession (Isakotsu Mountain). On Aoyama Street.]] :It measures 6 meters in length and is approximately 5 meters high; the height up to the {{ill|Roof|en|Roof|WD=Q83180|lt=Roof|1=Roof}} is about two-thirds that of the floats used in Kyoto’s Gion Festival, clearly reflecting Kyoto’s influence. The original Hoko Mountain was smaller and simpler than the current one; however, around the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, it evolved to its present scale and gained in splendor. It is said that the first Hoko Mountain was constructed in {{ill|1663|en|1663|WD=Q7577|lt=1663|1=1663}} ({{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun|1=Kanbun (era)}} 3) on Mikasa Mountain in Kamigawara Town. ({{ill|Tamba-Sasayama municipal designation|de|Städtisches Kulturgut Tamba-Sasayama|ja|篠山市指定文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamba-Sasayama municipal designation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) *Upper Town – 4 units : Mikasa Mountain (Kamigawara Town), Phoenix Mountain (Shimogawara Town), Peacock Mountain (Kamitachi Town), Takasago Mountain (Shimotachi Town) *Lower Town – 5 units : Kenhoko Mountain (Gofukuchō), Shōshō Mountain (Kaminikaimachō), Isakotsu Mountain (Shimonikaimachō), Sotetsu Mountain (Uoyachō), Kagami Mountain (Nishimachō) ;Mikoshi :The mikoshi were donated and enshrined in {{ill|1694|en|1694|WD=Q7694|lt=1694|1=1694}} ({{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku|1=Genroku}} 7) and {{ill|1779|en|1779|WD=Q7714|lt=1779|1=1779}} ({{ill|An'ei|en|An'ei|WD=Q1193910|lt=An'ei|1=An'ei}} 8), respectively. The presence of four mikoshi corresponds to the four festival deities of Kasuga Jinja – namely, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|en|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}, {{ill|Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|武甕槌命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to タケミカヅチ|1=Takemikazuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1502690}}, {{ill|Futsunushi-no-kami|de|Futsunushi-no-kami|ja|経津主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi-no-kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 経津主神|1=Futsunushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107286}}, and {{ill|Himegami|en|Himegami|WD=Q22070227|lt=Hime-Ōkami|1=Himegami}}. [[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Tambasasayama, Hyogo)]][[ja:春日神社 (丹波篠山市)]][[ko:가스가 신사 (단바사사야마시)]] [[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Tambasasayama, Hyogo)]][[ja:春日神社 (丹波篠山市)]][[ko:가스가 신사 (단바사사야마시)]] [[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Tambasasayama, Hyogo)]][[ja:春日神社 (丹波篠山市)]][[ko:가스가 신사 (단바사사야마시)]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Tambasasayama, Hyogo)]][[ja:春日神社 (丹波篠山市)]][[ko:가스가 신사 (단바사사야마시)]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Kasuga Jinja (春日神社) | location = {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|Tamba-Sasayama|en|Tamba-Sasayama|WD=Q877169|lt=Tamba-Sasayama|1=Tamba-Sasayama}}黒岡75 | coordinates = {{Coord|35.07825|N|135.21883333333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | enshrined_deities = {{ill|Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|武甕槌命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to タケミカヅチ|1=Takemikazuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1502690}}
{{ill|Futsunushi-no-kami|de|Futsunushi-no-kami|ja|経津主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi-no-kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 経津主神|1=Futsunushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107286}}
{{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|en|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}
{{ill|Hime-Ōkami|de|Hime-Ōkami|ja|姫大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime-Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 比売神|1=Himegami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22070227}} | shrine_Rank = {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|de|Präfekturschrein|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | main_hall = | main_festival = 3rd Saturday and Sunday of {{ill|October|en|October|WD=Q124|lt=October|1=October}} | image = File:篠山春日神社本殿sasayama-kasugajinjaPA156109.jpg | map = {{Location map | Japan Hyōgo | lat_deg = 35 | lat_min = 04 | lat_sec = 41.7 | lon_deg = 135 | lon_min = 13 | lon_sec = 07.8 | label = Kasuga Jinja | position = center | width = 200 | float = center }} | prefecture= {{ill|Tamba-Sasayama|WD=Q877169|1=Tamba-Sasayama}} | founded_by= {{ill|Fujiwara no Mototsune|WD=Q1397196|1=Fujiwara no Mototsune}}; {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|WD=Q2994735|1=Fujiwara no Tokihira}} | deity= {{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}; {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}; {{ill|Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}}; {{ill|Takemikazuchi|WD=Q1502690|1=Takemikazuchi}} }} '''Kasuga Jinja''' (春日神社) is a Shinto shrine located at {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|Tamba-Sasayama|en|Tamba-Sasayama|WD=Q877169|lt=Tamba-Sasayama|1=Tamba-Sasayama}}黒岡75. It was {{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|WD=Q195793|lt=Branch shrine|1=Bunrei}} of the {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga Grand Shrine|1=Kasuga-taisha}} in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}. Under the {{ill|Former shrine Ranking system|de|Ehemaliges Shintō-Rangsystem|ja|旧社格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Former shrine Ranking system|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}, it held the status of a {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|de|Präfekturschrein|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. ==Overview== It enshrines a branch of the {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga Grand Shrine|1=Kasuga-taisha}} of Nara, and locally the deity is known as "Okasuga-san" – an {{ill|Ujigami|en|Ujigami|WD=Q767655|lt=Ujigami|1=Ujigami}}. The spring {{ill|Kasuga Noh|de|Kasuga Nō|ja|春日能|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Noh|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} performed on the {{ill|Noh stage|de|Nō-Bühne|ja|能楽殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Noh stage|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the autumn {{ill|Party|en|Party|WD=Q200538|lt=Festival|1=Party}} held on the third Saturday and Sunday of {{ill|October|en|October|WD=Q124|lt=October|1=October}} are said to be modeled after the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|en|Gion Matsuri|WD=Q979873|lt=Gion Festival|1=Gion Matsuri}} of {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|WD=Q692714|lt=Yasaka|1=Yasaka Shrine}} in Kyoto. In this celebration, four mikoshi (portable shrines) and nine elaborately decorated yamahoko floats parade through an old town that retains the atmosphere of "Little Kyoto" with the resonant sounds of flutes ({{ill|Flute|en|Flute|WD=Q11405|lt=Fue|1=Flute}}) and bells ({{ill|Bell|en|Bell|WD=Q101401|lt=Kane (instrument)|1=Bell}}), evoking a charm regarded as more atmospheric than the modern rendition of Kyoto’s Gion Festival. ==History== In the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, in {{ill|876|en|876|WD=Q23815|lt=876|1=876}} ({{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|WD=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan|1=Zhenguan}} 18), Fujiwara no Mototsune and his son Fujiwara no Tokihira, then lords of Hioki Manor, enshrined a branch of the {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga Grand Shrine|1=Kasuga-taisha}} (located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}) – the guardian deity of the Fujiwara clan – in a small hill in the southern part of Kurooka Village (then known as Sasayama, where {{ill|Sasayama Castle|en|Sasayama Castle|WD=Q2760134|lt=Sasayama Castle|1=Sasayama Castle}} now stands). This is considered the origin of the shrine. Later, in {{ill|1609|en|1609|WD=Q6762|lt=1609|1=1609}} ({{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō|1=Keichō}} 14), during the construction of Sasayama Castle, the shrine was relocated to its present site. The Kanjin-kō, which involved relocating the four {{ill|Shinrei|de|Shinrei|ja|神霊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinrei|ja_comment=jawiki s to 霊魂|1=Soul|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9165}} used for the autumn festival, saw two of these donated from Kurooka Village in {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku|1=Genroku}} 7 during {{ill|June|en|June|WD=Q120|lt=June|1=June}}, while the other two were contributed from Sasayama Town in {{ill|An'ei|en|An'ei|WD=Q1193910|lt=An'ei|1=An'ei}} 8 during {{ill|September|en|September|WD=Q123|lt=September|1=September}} (although there is also a theory placing it in Genroku 13). The nine floats, known as the “Chinchiki-yama,” were donated by various local communities from {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun|1=Kanbun (era)}} 3 onward. They were modeled after the floats of Kyoto’s Gion Festival and are noted for their splendid, luxurious appearance. Until the late {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, floats adorned with motifs such as phoenixes, peacocks, cycads, and shōjō were paraded; however, the advent of {{ill|Electrical cable|en|Electrical cable|WD=Q188447|lt=Electric wires|1=Electrical cable}} eventually necessitated floats without such adornments. In addition, the {{ill|Taiko drum float|de|Taiko-Festwagen|ja|太鼓御興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiko drum float|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is said to have originated during the {{ill|Culture and Bunsei eras|de|Bunka/Bunsei|ja|文化・文政|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Culture and Bunsei eras|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, when potters of {{ill|Ōjiyama ware|de|Ōjiyama-Keramik|ja|王地山焼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōjiyama ware|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} first began carrying it. ;Hoko Mountain [[File:春日神社山鉾巡行PA160707.jpg|thumbnail|180px|Yamahoko Procession (Isakotsu Mountain). On Aoyama Street.]] :It measures 6 meters in length and is approximately 5 meters high; the height up to the {{ill|Roof|en|Roof|WD=Q83180|lt=Roof|1=Roof}} is about two-thirds that of the floats used in Kyoto’s Gion Festival, clearly reflecting Kyoto’s influence. The original Hoko Mountain was smaller and simpler than the current one; however, around the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, it evolved to its present scale and gained in splendor. It is said that the first Hoko Mountain was constructed in {{ill|1663|en|1663|WD=Q7577|lt=1663|1=1663}} ({{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun|1=Kanbun (era)}} 3) on Mikasa Mountain in Kamigawara Town. ({{ill|Tamba-Sasayama municipal designation|de|Städtisches Kulturgut Tamba-Sasayama|ja|篠山市指定文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamba-Sasayama municipal designation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) *Upper Town – 4 units : Mikasa Mountain (Kamigawara Town), Phoenix Mountain (Shimogawara Town), Peacock Mountain (Kamitachi Town), Takasago Mountain (Shimotachi Town) *Lower Town – 5 units : Kenhoko Mountain (Gofukuchō), Shōshō Mountain (Kaminikaimachō), Isakotsu Mountain (Shimonikaimachō), Sotetsu Mountain (Uoyachō), Kagami Mountain (Nishimachō) ;Mikoshi :The mikoshi were donated and enshrined in {{ill|1694|en|1694|WD=Q7694|lt=1694|1=1694}} ({{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku|1=Genroku}} 7) and {{ill|1779|en|1779|WD=Q7714|lt=1779|1=1779}} ({{ill|An'ei|en|An'ei|WD=Q1193910|lt=An'ei|1=An'ei}} 8), respectively. The presence of four mikoshi corresponds to the four festival deities of Kasuga Jinja – namely, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|en|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}, {{ill|Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|武甕槌命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to タケミカヅチ|1=Takemikazuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1502690}}, {{ill|Futsunushi-no-kami|de|Futsunushi-no-kami|ja|経津主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi-no-kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 経津主神|1=Futsunushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107286}}, and {{ill|Himegami|en|Himegami|WD=Q22070227|lt=Hime-Ōkami|1=Himegami}}. File:2023-10-14 春日神社山鉾巡行 DSCF5890.jpg|Yamahoko Procession (Isakotsu Mountain). On Aoyama Street. File:2023-10-15 春日神社山鉾巡行 Sasayama Castle Town DSCF6020.jpg|Yamahoko Procession (Phoenix Mountain). In Nikaimachi. File:2023-10-15 春日神社山鉾巡行 DSCF6006.jpg|Yamahoko Procession (Phoenix Mountain). In Nikaimachi. File:2023-10-14 春日神社山鉾巡行 DSCF5989.jpg|Yamahoko Procession. In front of Kasuga Jinja. ==Shrine Buildings== * {{ill|Honden|en|Honden|WD=Q2044994|lt=Main Hall|1=Honden}} * {{ill|Noh stage|de|Nō-Bühne|ja|能楽殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Noh stage|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} [[File:春日神社ー篠山ー翁奉納P1011774.jpg|thumbnail|180px|Okina Dedication (2006.1.1)]] :The Noh stage was built in {{ill|1861|en|1861|WD=Q7693|lt=1861|1=1861}} (the first year of {{ill|Bunkyū|en|Bunkyū|WD=Q730266|lt=Bunkyū|1=Bunkyū}}) during the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|WD=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu|1=Bakumatsu}} by Aoyama Tadayoshi, the 13th lord of the Sasayama domain. Known as a Noh enthusiast, he dedicated this stage as an offering. The architectural work was executed by Inayama Kasha and Nagai Rihei, while the pine paintings in the stage background were created by Matsuoka Sōemon; at the time, it was acclaimed as the finest Noh stage west of {{ill|Hakone|de|Hakone|ja|箱根|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakone|1=Hakone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603342}}. To enhance the resonance of footsteps on the floorboards, a large jar was installed underneath—and its perfection is considered among the best in the nation. On {{ill|January 1|en|January 1|WD=Q2150|lt=January 1|1=January 1}} during New Year in Japan, the Okina dedication is held, and every year in early {{ill|April|en|April|WD=Q118|lt=April|1=April}}, when the {{ill|Sakura (disambiguation)|en|Sakura (disambiguation)|WD=Q225112|lt=Sakura|1=Sakura (disambiguation)}} bloom, the "Sasayama Kasuga Noh" is staged. In {{ill|2003|en|2003|WD=Q1986|lt=2003|1=2003}} (Heisei 15), it was designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.篠山市教育委員会 現地説明看板 * {{ill|Ema Hall|de|Ema-Halle|ja|絵馬堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema Hall|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} :Constructed in {{ill|1952|en|1952|WD=Q5272|lt=1952|1=1952}} ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 27) August as a memorial to peace, the Ema Hall houses a horse painting that originally hung in the old prayer hall. In {{ill|Keian|en|Keian|WD=Q1146135|lt=Keian|1=Keian}} 2, Matsudaira Tadakuni (the lord of the Akashi Domain in Harima Province) dedicated a finely detailed Black Divine Horse by Kano Naonobu. Owing to a local legend that at night the horse would escape the painting to devour beans in the Kurooka fields, a metal net was installed exclusively on this ema. This Black Divine Horse has also been designated as a treasured cultural property. ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Property (National)=== * Noh stage ===Tamba-Sasayama Municipal Cultural Properties=== * Black Divine Horse painting ==Annual Events== * {{ill|January 1|en|January 1|WD=Q2150|lt=January 1|1=January 1}} – Okina dedication; held shortly after midnight. * {{ill|Early [[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Tambasasayama, Hyogo)]][[ja:春日神社 (丹波篠山市)]][[ko:가스가 신사 (단바사사야마시)]] [[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Tambasasayama, Hyogo)]][[ja:春日神社 (丹波篠山市)]][[ko:가스가 신사 (단바사사야마시)]] [[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Tambasasayama, Hyogo)]][[ja:春日神社 (丹波篠山市)]][[ko:가스가 신사 (단바사사야마시)]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Tambasasayama, Hyogo)]][[ja:春日神社 (丹波篠山市)]][[ko:가스가 신사 (단바사사야마시)]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Kasuga Jinja (春日神社) | location = {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|Tamba-Sasayama|en|Tamba-Sasayama|WD=Q877169|lt=Tamba-Sasayama|1=Tamba-Sasayama}}黒岡75 | coordinates = {{Coord|35.07825|N|135.21883333333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | enshrined_deities = {{ill|Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|武甕槌命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to タケミカヅチ|1=Takemikazuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1502690}}
{{ill|Futsunushi-no-kami|de|Futsunushi-no-kami|ja|経津主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi-no-kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 経津主神|1=Futsunushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107286}}
{{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|en|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}
{{ill|Hime-Ōkami|de|Hime-Ōkami|ja|姫大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime-Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 比売神|1=Himegami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22070227}} | shrine_Rank = {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|de|Präfekturschrein|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | main_hall = | main_festival = 3rd Saturday and Sunday of {{ill|October|en|October|WD=Q124|lt=October|1=October}} | image = File:篠山春日神社本殿sasayama-kasugajinjaPA156109.jpg | map = {{Location map | Japan Hyōgo | lat_deg = 35 | lat_min = 04 | lat_sec = 41.7 | lon_deg = 135 | lon_min = 13 | lon_sec = 07.8 | label = Kasuga Jinja | position = center | width = 200 | float = center }} | prefecture= {{ill|Tamba-Sasayama|WD=Q877169|1=Tamba-Sasayama}} | founded_by= {{ill|Fujiwara no Mototsune|WD=Q1397196|1=Fujiwara no Mototsune}}; {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|WD=Q2994735|1=Fujiwara no Tokihira}} | deity= {{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}; {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}; {{ill|Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}}; {{ill|Takemikazuchi|WD=Q1502690|1=Takemikazuchi}} }} '''Kasuga Jinja''' (春日神社) is a Shinto shrine located at {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|Tamba-Sasayama|en|Tamba-Sasayama|WD=Q877169|lt=Tamba-Sasayama|1=Tamba-Sasayama}}黒岡75. It was {{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|WD=Q195793|lt=Branch shrine|1=Bunrei}} of the {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga Grand Shrine|1=Kasuga-taisha}} in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}. Under the {{ill|Former shrine Ranking system|de|Ehemaliges Shintō-Rangsystem|ja|旧社格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Former shrine Ranking system|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}, it held the status of a {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|de|Präfekturschrein|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. ==Overview== It enshrines a branch of the {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga Grand Shrine|1=Kasuga-taisha}} of Nara, and locally the deity is known as "Okasuga-san" – an {{ill|Ujigami|en|Ujigami|WD=Q767655|lt=Ujigami|1=Ujigami}}. The spring {{ill|Kasuga Noh|de|Kasuga Nō|ja|春日能|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Noh|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} performed on the {{ill|Noh stage|de|Nō-Bühne|ja|能楽殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Noh stage|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the autumn {{ill|Party|en|Party|WD=Q200538|lt=Festival|1=Party}} held on the third Saturday and Sunday of {{ill|October|en|October|WD=Q124|lt=October|1=October}} are said to be modeled after the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|en|Gion Matsuri|WD=Q979873|lt=Gion Festival|1=Gion Matsuri}} of {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|WD=Q692714|lt=Yasaka|1=Yasaka Shrine}} in Kyoto. In this celebration, four mikoshi (portable shrines) and nine elaborately decorated yamahoko floats parade through an old town that retains the atmosphere of "Little Kyoto" with the resonant sounds of flutes ({{ill|Flute|en|Flute|WD=Q11405|lt=Fue|1=Flute}}) and bells ({{ill|Bell|en|Bell|WD=Q101401|lt=Kane (instrument)|1=Bell}}), evoking a charm regarded as more atmospheric than the modern rendition of Kyoto’s Gion Festival. ==History== In the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, in {{ill|876|en|876|WD=Q23815|lt=876|1=876}} ({{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|WD=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan|1=Zhenguan}} 18), Fujiwara no Mototsune and his son Fujiwara no Tokihira, then lords of Hioki Manor, enshrined a branch of the {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga Grand Shrine|1=Kasuga-taisha}} (located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}) – the guardian deity of the Fujiwara clan – in a small hill in the southern part of Kurooka Village (then known as Sasayama, where {{ill|Sasayama Castle|en|Sasayama Castle|WD=Q2760134|lt=Sasayama Castle|1=Sasayama Castle}} now stands). This is considered the origin of the shrine. Later, in {{ill|1609|en|1609|WD=Q6762|lt=1609|1=1609}} ({{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō|1=Keichō}} 14), during the construction of Sasayama Castle, the shrine was relocated to its present site. The Kanjin-kō, which involved relocating the four {{ill|Shinrei|de|Shinrei|ja|神霊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinrei|ja_comment=jawiki s to 霊魂|1=Soul|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9165}} used for the autumn festival, saw two of these donated from Kurooka Village in {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku|1=Genroku}} 7 during {{ill|June|en|June|WD=Q120|lt=June|1=June}}, while the other two were contributed from Sasayama Town in {{ill|An'ei|en|An'ei|WD=Q1193910|lt=An'ei|1=An'ei}} 8 during {{ill|September|en|September|WD=Q123|lt=September|1=September}} (although there is also a theory placing it in Genroku 13). The nine floats, known as the “Chinchiki-yama,” were donated by various local communities from {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun|1=Kanbun (era)}} 3 onward. They were modeled after the floats of Kyoto’s Gion Festival and are noted for their splendid, luxurious appearance. Until the late {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, floats adorned with motifs such as phoenixes, peacocks, cycads, and shōjō were paraded; however, the advent of {{ill|Electrical cable|en|Electrical cable|WD=Q188447|lt=Electric wires|1=Electrical cable}} eventually necessitated floats without such adornments. In addition, the {{ill|Taiko drum float|de|Taiko-Festwagen|ja|太鼓御興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiko drum float|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is said to have originated during the {{ill|Culture and Bunsei eras|de|Bunka/Bunsei|ja|文化・文政|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Culture and Bunsei eras|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, when potters of {{ill|Ōjiyama ware|de|Ōjiyama-Keramik|ja|王地山焼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōjiyama ware|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} first began carrying it. ;Hoko Mountain [[File:春日神社山鉾巡行PA160707.jpg|thumbnail|180px|Yamahoko Procession (Isakotsu Mountain). On Aoyama Street.]] :It measures 6 meters in length and is approximately 5 meters high; the height up to the {{ill|Roof|en|Roof|WD=Q83180|lt=Roof|1=Roof}} is about two-thirds that of the floats used in Kyoto’s Gion Festival, clearly reflecting Kyoto’s influence. The original Hoko Mountain was smaller and simpler than the current one; however, around the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, it evolved to its present scale and gained in splendor. It is said that the first Hoko Mountain was constructed in {{ill|1663|en|1663|WD=Q7577|lt=1663|1=1663}} ({{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun|1=Kanbun (era)}} 3) on Mikasa Mountain in Kamigawara Town. ({{ill|Tamba-Sasayama municipal designation|de|Städtisches Kulturgut Tamba-Sasayama|ja|篠山市指定文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamba-Sasayama municipal designation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) *Upper Town – 4 units : Mikasa Mountain (Kamigawara Town), Phoenix Mountain (Shimogawara Town), Peacock Mountain (Kamitachi Town), Takasago Mountain (Shimotachi Town) *Lower Town – 5 units : Kenhoko Mountain (Gofukuchō), Shōshō Mountain (Kaminikaimachō), Isakotsu Mountain (Shimonikaimachō), Sotetsu Mountain (Uoyachō), Kagami Mountain (Nishimachō) ;Mikoshi :The mikoshi were donated and enshrined in {{ill|1694|en|1694|WD=Q7694|lt=1694|1=1694}} ({{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku|1=Genroku}} 7) and {{ill|1779|en|1779|WD=Q7714|lt=1779|1=1779}} ({{ill|An'ei|en|An'ei|WD=Q1193910|lt=An'ei|1=An'ei}} 8), respectively. The presence of four mikoshi corresponds to the four festival deities of Kasuga Jinja – namely, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|en|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}, {{ill|Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|武甕槌命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to タケミカヅチ|1=Takemikazuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1502690}}, {{ill|Futsunushi-no-kami|de|Futsunushi-no-kami|ja|経津主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi-no-kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 経津主神|1=Futsunushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107286}}, and {{ill|Himegami|en|Himegami|WD=Q22070227|lt=Hime-Ōkami|1=Himegami}}. File:2023-10-14 春日神社山鉾巡行 DSCF5890.jpg|Yamahoko Procession (Isakotsu Mountain). On Aoyama Street. File:2023-10-15 春日神社山鉾巡行 Sasayama Castle Town DSCF6020.jpg|Yamahoko Procession (Phoenix Mountain). In Nikaimachi. File:2023-10-15 春日神社山鉾巡行 DSCF6006.jpg|Yamahoko Procession (Phoenix Mountain). In Nikaimachi. File:2023-10-14 春日神社山鉾巡行 DSCF5989.jpg|Yamahoko Procession. In front of Kasuga Jinja. ==Shrine Buildings== * {{ill|Honden|en|Honden|WD=Q2044994|lt=Main Hall|1=Honden}} * {{ill|Noh stage|de|Nō-Bühne|ja|能楽殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Noh stage|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} [[File:春日神社ー篠山ー翁奉納P1011774.jpg|thumbnail|180px|Okina Dedication (2006.1.1)]] :The Noh stage was built in {{ill|1861|en|1861|WD=Q7693|lt=1861|1=1861}} (the first year of {{ill|Bunkyū|en|Bunkyū|WD=Q730266|lt=Bunkyū|1=Bunkyū}}) during the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|WD=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu|1=Bakumatsu}} by Aoyama Tadayoshi, the 13th lord of the Sasayama domain. Known as a Noh enthusiast, he dedicated this stage as an offering. The architectural work was executed by Inayama Kasha and Nagai Rihei, while the pine paintings in the stage background were created by Matsuoka Sōemon; at the time, it was acclaimed as the finest Noh stage west of {{ill|Hakone|de|Hakone|ja|箱根|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakone|1=Hakone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603342}}. To enhance the resonance of footsteps on the floorboards, a large jar was installed underneath—and its perfection is considered among the best in the nation. On {{ill|January 1|en|January 1|WD=Q2150|lt=January 1|1=January 1}} during New Year in Japan, the Okina dedication is held, and every year in early {{ill|April|en|April|WD=Q118|lt=April|1=April}}, when the {{ill|Sakura (disambiguation)|en|Sakura (disambiguation)|WD=Q225112|lt=Sakura|1=Sakura (disambiguation)}} bloom, the "Sasayama Kasuga Noh" is staged. In {{ill|2003|en|2003|WD=Q1986|lt=2003|1=2003}} (Heisei 15), it was designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.篠山市教育委員会 現地説明看板 * {{ill|Ema Hall|de|Ema-Halle|ja|絵馬堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema Hall|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} :Constructed in {{ill|1952|en|1952|WD=Q5272|lt=1952|1=1952}} ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 27) August as a memorial to peace, the Ema Hall houses a horse painting that originally hung in the old prayer hall. In {{ill|Keian|en|Keian|WD=Q1146135|lt=Keian|1=Keian}} 2, Matsudaira Tadakuni (the lord of the Akashi Domain in Harima Province) dedicated a finely detailed Black Divine Horse by Kano Naonobu. Owing to a local legend that at night the horse would escape the painting to devour beans in the Kurooka fields, a metal net was installed exclusively on this ema. This Black Divine Horse has also been designated as a treasured cultural property. ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Property (National)=== * Noh stage ===Tamba-Sasayama Municipal Cultural Properties=== * Black Divine Horse painting ==Annual Events== * {{ill|January 1|en|January 1|WD=Q2150|lt=January 1|1=January 1}} – Okina dedication; held shortly after midnight. * {{ill|Early April|de|Aprilanfang|ja|4月上旬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Early April|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} – Kasuga Noh. * {{ill|Third weekend of October|de|Drittes Oktoberwochenende|ja|10月第3土・日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Third weekend of October|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} – Autumn festival. ==Access== * {{ill|Rail transport in Japan|en|Rail transport in Japan|WD=Q1099973|lt=Rail transport in Japan|1=Rail transport in Japan}}: JR Fukuchiyama Line – “{{ill|Sasayamaguchi Station|en|Sasayamaguchi Station|WD=Q966175|lt=Sasayamaguchi Station|1=Sasayamaguchi Station}}” * {{ill|Bus (disambiguation)|en|Bus (disambiguation)|WD=Q774740|lt=Bus transport in Japan|1=Bus (disambiguation)}}: Wing Kamiki (formerly Kamiki Green Bus) – a short walk from “In front of Kasuga Jinja” ==Notes== ==See also== * {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|en|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} * {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|Takemikazuchi|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} * {{ill|Futsunushi|en|Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-kami|1=Futsunushi}} * {{ill|Himegami|en|Himegami|WD=Q22070227|lt=Hime-Ōkami|1=Himegami}} * {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (deity)|de|Kasuga (Gottheit)|ja|春日神|1=Kasuga-no-Kami|2=ja|3=春日神|4=fr|5=Kasuga no Kami|6=zh|7=春日權現|8=wuu|9=春日權現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Shrine (deity)|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga-no-Kami|qq=}} ==External links== * {{Official website|http://kasuga.tanba-sasayama.net/|丹波篠山 {{ill|Futsunushi-no-kami|de|Futsunushi-no-kami|ja|経津主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi-no-kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 経津主神|1=Futsunushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107286}}
{{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|en|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}
{{ill|Hime-Ōkami|de|Hime-Ōkami|ja|姫大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime-Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 比売神|1=Himegami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22070227}} | shrine_Rank = {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|de|Präfekturschrein|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | main_hall = | main_festival = 3rd Saturday and Sunday of {{ill|October|en|October|WD=Q124|lt=October|1=October}} | image = File:篠山春日神社本殿sasayama-kasugajinjaPA156109.jpg | map = {{Location map | Japan Hyōgo | lat_deg = 35 | lat_min = 04 | lat_sec = 41.7 | lon_deg = 135 | lon_min = 13 | lon_sec = 07.8 | label = Kasuga Jinja | position = center | width = 200 | float = center }} | prefecture= {{ill|Tamba-Sasayama|WD=Q877169|1=Tamba-Sasayama}} | founded_by= {{ill|Fujiwara no Mototsune|WD=Q1397196|1=Fujiwara no Mototsune}}; {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|WD=Q2994735|1=Fujiwara no Tokihira}} | deity= {{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}; {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}; {{ill|Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}}; {{ill|Takemikazuchi|WD=Q1502690|1=Takemikazuchi}} }} '''Kasuga Jinja''' (春日神社) is a Shinto shrine located at {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|Tamba-Sasayama|en|Tamba-Sasayama|WD=Q877169|lt=Tamba-Sasayama|1=Tamba-Sasayama}}黒岡75. It was {{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|WD=Q195793|lt=Branch shrine|1=Bunrei}} of the {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga Grand Shrine|1=Kasuga-taisha}} in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}. Under the {{ill|Former shrine Ranking system|de|Ehemaliges Shintō-Rangsystem|ja|旧社格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Former shrine Ranking system|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}, it held the status of a {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|de|Präfekturschrein|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. ==Overview== It enshrines a branch of the {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga Grand Shrine|1=Kasuga-taisha}} of Nara, and locally the deity is known as "Okasuga-san" – an {{ill|Ujigami|en|Ujigami|WD=Q767655|lt=Ujigami|1=Ujigami}}. The spring {{ill|Kasuga Noh|de|Kasuga Nō|ja|春日能|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Noh|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} performed on the {{ill|Noh stage|de|Nō-Bühne|ja|能楽殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Noh stage|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the autumn {{ill|Party|en|Party|WD=Q200538|lt=Festival|1=Party}} held on the third Saturday and Sunday of {{ill|October|en|October|WD=Q124|lt=October|1=October}} are said to be modeled after the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|en|Gion Matsuri|WD=Q979873|lt=Gion Festival|1=Gion Matsuri}} of {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|WD=Q692714|lt=Yasaka|1=Yasaka Shrine}} in Kyoto. In this celebration, four mikoshi (portable shrines) and nine elaborately decorated yamahoko floats parade through an old town that retains the atmosphere of "Little Kyoto" with the resonant sounds of flutes ({{ill|Flute|en|Flute|WD=Q11405|lt=Fue|1=Flute}}) and bells ({{ill|Bell|en|Bell|WD=Q101401|lt=Kane (instrument)|1=Bell}}), evoking a charm regarded as more atmospheric than the modern rendition of Kyoto’s Gion Festival. ==History== In the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, in {{ill|876|en|876|WD=Q23815|lt=876|1=876}} ({{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|WD=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan|1=Zhenguan}} 18), Fujiwara no Mototsune and his son Fujiwara no Tokihira, then lords of Hioki Manor, enshrined a branch of the {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga Grand Shrine|1=Kasuga-taisha}} (located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}) – the guardian deity of the Fujiwara clan – in a small hill in the southern part of Kurooka Village (then known as Sasayama, where {{ill|Sasayama Castle|en|Sasayama Castle|WD=Q2760134|lt=Sasayama Castle|1=Sasayama Castle}} now stands). This is considered the origin of the shrine. Later, in {{ill|1609|en|1609|WD=Q6762|lt=1609|1=1609}} ({{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō|1=Keichō}} 14), during the construction of Sasayama Castle, the shrine was relocated to its present site. The Kanjin-kō, which involved relocating the four {{ill|Shinrei|de|Shinrei|ja|神霊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinrei|ja_comment=jawiki s to 霊魂|1=Soul|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9165}} used for the autumn festival, saw two of these donated from Kurooka Village in {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku|1=Genroku}} 7 during {{ill|June|en|June|WD=Q120|lt=June|1=June}}, while the other two were contributed from Sasayama Town in {{ill|An'ei|en|An'ei|WD=Q1193910|lt=An'ei|1=An'ei}} 8 during {{ill|September|en|September|WD=Q123|lt=September|1=September}} (although there is also a theory placing it in Genroku 13). The nine floats, known as the “Chinchiki-yama,” were donated by various local communities from {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun|1=Kanbun (era)}} 3 onward. They were modeled after the floats of Kyoto’s Gion Festival and are noted for their splendid, luxurious appearance. Until the late {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, floats adorned with motifs such as phoenixes, peacocks, cycads, and shōjō were paraded; however, the advent of {{ill|Electrical cable|en|Electrical cable|WD=Q188447|lt=Electric wires|1=Electrical cable}} eventually necessitated floats without such adornments. In addition, the {{ill|Taiko drum float|de|Taiko-Festwagen|ja|太鼓御興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiko drum float|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is said to have originated during the {{ill|Culture and Bunsei eras|de|Bunka/Bunsei|ja|文化・文政|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Culture and Bunsei eras|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, when potters of {{ill|Ōjiyama ware|de|Ōjiyama-Keramik|ja|王地山焼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōjiyama ware|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} first began carrying it. ;Hoko Mountain [[File:春日神社山鉾巡行PA160707.jpg|thumbnail|180px|Yamahoko Procession (Isakotsu Mountain). On Aoyama Street.]] :It measures 6 meters in length and is approximately 5 meters high; the height up to the {{ill|Roof|en|Roof|WD=Q83180|lt=Roof|1=Roof}} is about two-thirds that of the floats used in Kyoto’s Gion Festival, clearly reflecting Kyoto’s influence. The original Hoko Mountain was smaller and simpler than the current one; however, around the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, it evolved to its present scale and gained in splendor. It is said that the first Hoko Mountain was constructed in {{ill|1663|en|1663|WD=Q7577|lt=1663|1=1663}} ({{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun|1=Kanbun (era)}} 3) on Mikasa Mountain in Kamigawara Town. ({{ill|Tamba-Sasayama municipal designation|de|Städtisches Kulturgut Tamba-Sasayama|ja|篠山市指定文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamba-Sasayama municipal designation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) *Upper Town – 4 units : Mikasa Mountain (Kamigawara Town), Phoenix Mountain (Shimogawara Town), Peacock Mountain (Kamitachi Town), Takasago Mountain (Shimotachi Town) *Lower Town – 5 units : Kenhoko Mountain (Gofukuchō), Shōshō Mountain (Kaminikaimachō), Isakotsu Mountain (Shimonikaimachō), Sotetsu Mountain (Uoyachō), Kagami Mountain (Nishimachō) ;Mikoshi :The mikoshi were donated and enshrined in {{ill|1694|en|1694|WD=Q7694|lt=1694|1=1694}} ({{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku|1=Genroku}} 7) and {{ill|1779|en|1779|WD=Q7714|lt=1779|1=1779}} ({{ill|An'ei|en|An'ei|WD=Q1193910|lt=An'ei|1=An'ei}} 8), respectively. The presence of four mikoshi corresponds to the four festival deities of Kasuga Jinja – namely, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|en|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}, {{ill|Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|武甕槌命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to タケミカヅチ|1=Takemikazuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1502690}}, {{ill|Futsunushi-no-kami|de|Futsunushi-no-kami|ja|経津主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi-no-kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 経津主神|1=Futsunushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107286}}, and {{ill|Himegami|en|Himegami|WD=Q22070227|lt=Hime-Ōkami|1=Himegami}}. File:2023-10-14 春日神社山鉾巡行 DSCF5890.jpg|Yamahoko Procession (Isakotsu Mountain). On Aoyama Street. File:2023-10-15 春日神社山鉾巡行 Sasayama Castle Town DSCF6020.jpg|Yamahoko Procession (Phoenix Mountain). In Nikaimachi. File:2023-10-15 春日神社山鉾巡行 DSCF6006.jpg|Yamahoko Procession (Phoenix Mountain). In Nikaimachi. File:2023-10-14 春日神社山鉾巡行 DSCF5989.jpg|Yamahoko Procession. In front of Kasuga Jinja. ==Shrine Buildings== * {{ill|Honden|en|Honden|WD=Q2044994|lt=Main Hall|1=Honden}} * {{ill|Noh stage|de|Nō-Bühne|ja|能楽殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Noh stage|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} [[File:春日神社ー篠山ー翁奉納P1011774.jpg|thumbnail|180px|Okina Dedication (2006.1.1)]] :The Noh stage was built in {{ill|1861|en|1861|WD=Q7693|lt=1861|1=1861}} (the first year of {{ill|Bunkyū|en|Bunkyū|WD=Q730266|lt=Bunkyū|1=Bunkyū}}) during the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|WD=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu|1=Bakumatsu}} by Aoyama Tadayoshi, the 13th lord of the Sasayama domain. Known as a Noh enthusiast, he dedicated this stage as an offering. The architectural work was executed by Inayama Kasha and Nagai Rihei, while the pine paintings in the stage background were created by Matsuoka Sōemon; at the time, it was acclaimed as the finest Noh stage west of {{ill|Hakone|de|Hakone|ja|箱根|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakone|1=Hakone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603342}}. To enhance the resonance of footsteps on the floorboards, a large jar was installed underneath—and its perfection is considered among the best in the nation. On {{ill|January 1|en|January 1|WD=Q2150|lt=January 1|1=January 1}} during New Year in Japan, the Okina dedication is held, and every year in early {{ill|April|en|April|WD=Q118|lt=April|1=April}}, when the {{ill|Sakura (disambiguation)|en|Sakura (disambiguation)|WD=Q225112|lt=Sakura|1=Sakura (disambiguation)}} bloom, the "Sasayama Kasuga Noh" is staged. In {{ill|2003|en|2003|WD=Q1986|lt=2003|1=2003}} (Heisei 15), it was designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.篠山市教育委員会 現地説明看板 * {{ill|Ema Hall|de|Ema-Halle|ja|絵馬堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema Hall|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} :Constructed in {{ill|1952|en|1952|WD=Q5272|lt=1952|1=1952}} ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 27) August as a memorial to peace, the Ema Hall houses a horse painting that originally hung in the old prayer hall. In {{ill|Keian|en|Keian|WD=Q1146135|lt=Keian|1=Keian}} 2, Matsudaira Tadakuni (the lord of the Akashi Domain in Harima Province) dedicated a finely detailed Black Divine Horse by Kano Naonobu. Owing to a local legend that at night the horse would escape the painting to devour beans in the Kurooka fields, a metal net was installed exclusively on this ema. This Black Divine Horse has also been designated as a treasured cultural property. ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Property (National)=== * Noh stage ===Tamba-Sasayama Municipal Cultural Properties=== * Black Divine Horse painting ==Annual Events== * {{ill|January 1|en|January 1|WD=Q2150|lt=January 1|1=January 1}} – Okina dedication; held shortly after midnight. * {{ill|Early April|de|Aprilanfang|ja|4月上旬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Early April|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} – Kasuga Noh. * {{ill|Third weekend of October|de|Drittes Oktoberwochenende|ja|10月第3土・日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Third weekend of October|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} – Autumn festival. ==Access== * {{ill|Rail transport in Japan|en|Rail transport in Japan|WD=Q1099973|lt=Rail transport in Japan|1=Rail transport in Japan}}: JR Fukuchiyama Line – “{{ill|Sasayamaguchi Station|en|Sasayamaguchi Station|WD=Q966175|lt=Sasayamaguchi Station|1=Sasayamaguchi Station}}” * {{ill|Bus (disambiguation)|en|Bus (disambiguation)|WD=Q774740|lt=Bus transport in Japan|1=Bus (disambiguation)}}: Wing Kamiki (formerly Kamiki Green Bus) – a short walk from “In front of Kasuga Jinja” ==Notes== ==See also== * {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|en|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} * {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|Takemikazuchi|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} * {{ill|Futsunushi|en|Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-kami|1=Futsunushi}} * {{ill|Himegami|en|Himegami|WD=Q22070227|lt=Hime-Ōkami|1=Himegami}} * {{ill|Kasuga Shrine (deity)|de|Kasuga (Gottheit)|ja|春日神|1=Kasuga-no-Kami|2=ja|3=春日神|4=fr|5=Kasuga no Kami|6=zh|7=春日權現|8=wuu|9=春日權現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Shrine (deity)|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga-no-Kami|qq=}} ==External links== * {{Official website|http://kasuga.tanba-sasayama.net/|丹波篠山 rot}} | date=January 2020 * [https://tourism.sasayama.jp/association/ Tanba Sasayama Tourism Association Official Website] * {{ill|Futsunushi-no-kami|de|Futsunushi-no-kami|ja|経津主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi-no-kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 経津主神|1=Futsunushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107286}}
{{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|en|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}
{{ill|Hime-Ōkami|de|Hime-Ōkami|ja|姫大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime-Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 比売神|1=Himegami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22070227}} | shrine_Rank = {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|de|Präfekturschrein|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | main_hall = | main_festival = 3rd Saturday and Sunday of {{ill|October|en|October|WD=Q124|lt=October|1=October}} | image = File:篠山春日神社本殿sasayama-kasugajinjaPA156109.jpg | map = {{Location map | Japan Hyōgo | lat_deg = 35 | lat_min = 04 | lat_sec = 41.7 | lon_deg = 135 | lon_min = 13 | lon_sec = 07.8 | label = Kasuga Jinja | position = center | width = 200 | float = center }} | prefecture= {{ill|Tamba-Sasayama|WD=Q877169|1=Tamba-Sasayama}} | founded_by= {{ill|Fujiwara no Mototsune|WD=Q1397196|1=Fujiwara no Mototsune}}; {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|WD=Q2994735|1=Fujiwara no Tokihira}} | deity= {{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}; {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}; {{ill|Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}}; {{ill|Takemikazuchi|WD=Q1502690|1=Takemikazuchi}} }} '''Kasuga Jinja''' (春日神社) is a Shinto shrine located at {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|Tamba-Sasayama|en|Tamba-Sasayama|WD=Q877169|lt=Tamba-Sasayama|1=Tamba-Sasayama}}黒岡75. It was {{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|WD=Q195793|lt=Branch shrine|1=Bunrei}} of the {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga Grand Shrine|1=Kasuga-taisha}} in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}. Under the {{ill|Former shrine Ranking system|de|Ehemaliges Shintō-Rangsystem|ja|旧社格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Former shrine Ranking system|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}, it held the status of a {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|de|Präfekturschrein|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. ==Overview== It enshrines a branch of the {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga Grand Shrine|1=Kasuga-taisha}} of Nara, and locally the deity is known as "Okasuga-san" – an {{ill|Ujigami|en|Ujigami|WD=Q767655|lt=Ujigami|1=Ujigami}}. The spring {{ill|Kasuga Noh|de|Kasuga Nō|ja|春日能|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Noh|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} performed on the {{ill|Noh stage|de|Nō-Bühne|ja|能楽殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Noh stage|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the autumn {{ill|Party|en|Party|WD=Q200538|lt=Festival|1=Party}} held on the third Saturday and Sunday of {{ill|October|en|October|WD=Q124|lt=October|1=October}} are said to be modeled after the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|en|Gion Matsuri|WD=Q979873|lt=Gion Festival|1=Gion Matsuri}} of {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|WD=Q692714|lt=Yasaka|1=Yasaka Shrine}} in Kyoto. In this celebration, four mikoshi (portable shrines) and nine elaborately decorated yamahoko floats parade through an old town that retains the atmosphere of "Little Kyoto" with the resonant sounds of flutes ({{ill|Flute|en|Flute|WD=Q11405|lt=Fue|1=Flute}}) and bells ({{ill|Bell|en|Bell|WD=Q101401|lt=Kane (instrument)|1=Bell}}), evoking a charm regarded as more atmospheric than the modern rendition of Kyoto’s Gion Festival. ==History== In the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, in {{ill|876|en|876|WD=Q23815|lt=876|1=876}} ({{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|WD=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan|1=Zhenguan}} 18), Fujiwara no Mototsune and his son Fujiwara no Tokihira, then lords of Hioki Manor, enshrined a branch of the {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga Grand Shrine|1=Kasuga-taisha}} (located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}) – the guardian deity of the Fujiwara clan – in a small hill in the southern part of Kurooka Village (then known as Sasayama, where {{ill|Sasayama Castle|en|Sasayama Castle|WD=Q2760134|lt=Sasayama Castle|1=Sasayama Castle}} now stands). This is considered the origin of the shrine. Later, in {{ill|1609|en|1609|WD=Q6762|lt=1609|1=1609}} ({{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō|1=Keichō}} 14), during the construction of Sasayama Castle, the shrine was relocated to its present site. The Kanjin-kō, which involved relocating the four {{ill|Shinrei|de|Shinrei|ja|神霊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinrei|ja_comment=jawiki s to 霊魂|1=Soul|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9165}} used for the autumn festival, saw two of these donated from Kurooka Village in {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku|1=Genroku}} 7 during {{ill|June|en|June|WD=Q120|lt=June|1=June}}, while the other two were contributed from Sasayama Town in {{ill|An'ei|en|An'ei|WD=Q1193910|lt=An'ei|1=An'ei}} 8 during {{ill|September|en|September|WD=Q123|lt=September|1=September}} (although there is also a theory placing it in Genroku 13). The nine floats, known as the “Chinchiki-yama,” were donated by various local communities from {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun|1=Kanbun (era)}} 3 onward. They were modeled after the floats of Kyoto’s Gion Festival and are noted for their splendid, luxurious appearance. Until the late {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, floats adorned with motifs such as phoenixes, peacocks, cycads, and shōjō were paraded; however, the advent of {{ill|Electrical cable|en|Electrical cable|WD=Q188447|lt=Electric wires|1=Electrical cable}} eventually necessitated floats without such adornments. In addition, the {{ill|Taiko drum float|de|Taiko-Festwagen|ja|太鼓御興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiko drum float|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is said to have originated during the {{ill|Culture and Bunsei eras|de|Bunka/Bunsei|ja|文化・文政|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Culture and Bunsei eras|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, when potters of {{ill|Ōjiyama ware|de|Ōjiyama-Keramik|ja|王地山焼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōjiyama ware|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} first began carrying it. ;Hoko Mountain [[File:春日神社山鉾巡行PA160707.jpg|thumbnail|180px|Yamahoko Procession (Isakotsu Mountain). On Aoyama Street.]] :It measures 6 meters in length and is approximately 5 meters high; the height up to the {{ill|Roof|en|Roof|WD=Q83180|lt=Roof|1=Roof}} is about two-thirds that of the floats used in Kyoto’s Gion Festival, clearly reflecting Kyoto’s influence. The original Hoko Mountain was smaller and simpler than the current one; however, around the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, it evolved to its present scale and gained in splendor. It is said that the first Hoko Mountain was constructed in {{ill|1663|en|1663|WD=Q7577|lt=1663|1=1663}} ({{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun|1=Kanbun (era)}} 3) on Mikasa Mountain in Kamigawara Town. ({{ill|Tamba-Sasayama municipal designation|de|Städtisches Kulturgut Tamba-Sasayama|ja|篠山市指定文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamba-Sasayama municipal designation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) *Upper Town – 4 units : Mikasa Mountain (Kamigawara Town), Phoenix Mountain (Shimogawara Town), Peacock Mountain (Kamitachi Town), Takasago Mountain (Shimotachi Town) *Lower Town – 5 units : Kenhoko Mountain (Gofukuchō), Shōshō Mountain (Kaminikaimachō), Isakotsu Mountain (Shimonikaimachō), Sotetsu Mountain (Uoyachō), Kagami Mountain (Nishimachō) ;Mikoshi :The mikoshi were donated and enshrined in {{ill|1694|en|1694|WD=Q7694|lt=1694|1=1694}} ({{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku|1=Genroku}} 7) and {{ill|1779|en|1779|WD=Q7714|lt=1779|1=1779}} ({{ill|An'ei|en|An'ei|WD=Q1193910|lt=An'ei|1=An'ei}} 8), respectively. The presence of four mikoshi corresponds to the four festival deities of Kasuga Jinja – namely, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|en|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}, {{ill|Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|武甕槌命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to タケミカヅチ|1=Takemikazuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1502690}}, {{ill|Futsunushi-no-kami|de|Futsunushi-no-kami|ja|経津主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi-no-kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 経津主神|1=Futsunushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107286}}, and {{ill|Himegami|en|Himegami|WD=Q22070227|lt=Hime-Ōkami|1=Himegami}}. File:2023-10-14 春日神社山鉾巡行 DSCF5890.jpg|Yamahoko Procession (Isakotsu Mountain). On Aoyama Street. File:2023-10-15 春日神社山鉾巡行 Sasayama Castle Town DSCF6020.jpg|Yamahoko Procession (Phoenix Mountain). In Nikaimachi. File:2023-10-15 春日神社山鉾巡行 DSCF6006.jpg|Yamahoko Procession (Phoenix Mountain). In Nikaimachi. File:2023-10-14 春日神社山鉾巡行 DSCF5989.jpg|Yamahoko Procession. In front of Kasuga Jinja. ==Shrine Buildings== * {{ill|Honden|en|Honden|WD=Q2044994|lt=Main Hall|1=Honden}} * {{ill|Noh stage|de|Nō-Bühne|ja|能楽殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Noh stage|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} [[File:春日神社ー篠山ー翁奉納P1011774.jpg|thumbnail|180px|Okina Dedication (2006.1.1)]] :The Noh stage was built in {{ill|1861|en|1861|WD=Q7693|lt=1861|1=1861}} (the first year of {{ill|Bunkyū|en|Bunkyū|WD=Q730266|lt=Bunkyū|1=Bunkyū}}) during the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|WD=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu|1=Bakumatsu}} by Aoyama Tadayoshi, the 13th lord of the Sasayama domain. Known as a Noh enthusiast, he dedicated this stage as an offering. The architectural work was executed by Inayama Kasha and Nagai Rihei, while the pine paintings in the stage background were created by Matsuoka Sōemon; at the time, it was acclaimed as the finest Noh stage west of {{ill|Hakone|de|Hakone|ja|箱根|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakone|1=Hakone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603342}}. To enhance the resonance of footsteps on the floorboards, a large jar was installed underneath—and its perfection is considered among the best in the nation. On {{ill|January 1|en|January 1|WD=Q2150|lt=January 1|1=January 1}} during New Year in Japan, the Okina dedication is held, and every year in early {{ill|April|en|April|WD=Q118|lt=April|1=April}}, when the {{ill|Sakura (disambiguation)|en|Sakura (disambiguation)|WD=Q225112|lt=Sakura|1=Sakura (disambiguation)}} bloom, the "Sasayama Kasuga Noh" is staged. In {{ill|2003|en|2003|WD=Q1986|lt=2003|1=2003}} (Heisei 15), it was designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.篠山市教育委員会 現地説明看板 * {{ill|Ema Hall|de|Ema-Halle|ja|絵馬堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ema Hall|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} :Constructed in {{ill|1952|en|1952|WD=Q5272|lt=1952|1=1952}} ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 27) August as a memorial to peace, the Ema Hall houses a horse painting that originally hung in the old prayer hall. In {{ill|Keian|en|Keian|WD=Q1146135|lt=Keian|1=Keian}} 2, Matsudaira Tadakuni (the lord of the Akashi Domain in Harima Province) dedicated a finely detailed Black Divine Horse by Kano Naonobu. Owing to a local legend that at night the horse would escape the painting to devour beans in the Kurooka fields, a metal net was installed exclusively on this ema. This Black Divine Horse has also been designated as a treasured cultural property. ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Property (National)=== * Noh stage ===Tamba-Sasayama Municipal Cultural
